/home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F: 330 - 545
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


330:          DO s = 1, nnp%ang(ind)%n_symfgrp


331:             n_symf_s = nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s)%n_symf


332:             ! Per-thread persistent buffers grow lazily; THREADPRIVATE arrays sized together.


333:             IF (.NOT. ALLOCATED(scratch_sym) .OR. .NOT. ALLOCATED(local_y) .OR. &


334:                 scratch_max_sym < n_symf_s) THEN


335:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_sym)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_sym)


336:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_forcetmp)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_forcetmp)


337:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_force3tmp)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_force3tmp)


338:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_y)) DEALLOCATE(local_y)


339:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_fi)) DEALLOCATE(local_fi)


340:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_stress)) DEALLOCATE(local_stress)


341:                ALLOCATE(scratch_sym(n_symf_s), scratch_forcetmp(3, n_symf_s), &


342:                         scratch_force3tmp(3, 3, n_symf_s))


343:                ALLOCATE(local_y(n_symf_s), local_fi(3, n_symf_s), local_stress(3, 3, n_symf_s))


344:                scratch_max_sym = n_symf_s


345:             END IF


346:             symtmp => scratch_sym(1:n_symf_s)


347:             force3tmp => scratch_force3tmp(:, :, 1:n_symf_s)


348:             ! Reset thread-local accumulators


349:             local_y(1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


350:             local_fi(:, 1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


351:             IF (has_stress) local_stress(:, :, 1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


352: 


353:             ASSOCIATE (sg => nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s))


354:             ! Hoist k-loop invariants (fc1/fc2/dr1dx/dr2dx) — these scratch arrays are SHARED


355:             ! across threads.  One thread does the realloc check under !$OMP SINGLE; the


356:             ! precompute itself is then split across the team via !$OMP DO so every thread


357:             ! does its share of the spline evaluations instead of waiting on a single thread.


358:             !$OMP SINGLE


359:             fc_needed = neighbor%n_ang1(s)


360:             IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) fc_needed = MAX(fc_needed, neighbor%n_ang2(s))


361:             IF (scratch_max_fc < fc_needed) THEN


362:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_fc1)) &


363:                   DEALLOCATE(scratch_fc1, scratch_dfc1, scratch_fc2, scratch_dfc2, &


364:                              scratch_dr1dx, scratch_dr2dx)


365:                ALLOCATE(scratch_fc1(fc_needed), scratch_dfc1(fc_needed))


366:                ALLOCATE(scratch_fc2(fc_needed), scratch_dfc2(fc_needed))


367:                ALLOCATE(scratch_dr1dx(3, fc_needed), scratch_dr2dx(3, fc_needed))


368:                scratch_max_fc = fc_needed


369:             END IF


370:             !$OMP END SINGLE


371:             ! implicit barrier ensures the (re)allocation is visible to every thread


372:             SELECT CASE (cut_type)


373:             CASE (nnp_cut_cos)


374:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


375:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


376:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


377:                   fc_tmp = r1*sg%pi_over_cut


378:                   scratch_fc1(j) = 0.5_dp*(hermite_spline_value(spl_cos, fc_tmp, y1=dfc_tmp) + 1.0_dp)


379:                   scratch_dfc1(j) = 0.5_dp*dfc_tmp*sg%pi_over_cut


380:                   r1inv = 1.0_dp/r1


381:                   scratch_dr1dx(1, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(1, j, s)*r1inv


382:                   scratch_dr1dx(2, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(2, j, s)*r1inv


383:                   scratch_dr1dx(3, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(3, j, s)*r1inv


384:                END DO


385:                !$OMP END DO


386:                IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) THEN


387:                   !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


388:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


389:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


390:                      fc_tmp = r2*sg%pi_over_cut


391:                      scratch_fc2(k) = 0.5_dp*(hermite_spline_value(spl_cos, fc_tmp, y1=dfc_tmp) + 1.0_dp)


392:                      scratch_dfc2(k) = 0.5_dp*dfc_tmp*sg%pi_over_cut


393:                      r2inv = 1.0_dp/r2


394:                      scratch_dr2dx(1, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(1, k, s)*r2inv


395:                      scratch_dr2dx(2, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(2, k, s)*r2inv


396:                      scratch_dr2dx(3, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(3, k, s)*r2inv


397:                   END DO


398:                   !$OMP END DO


399:                END IF


400:             CASE (nnp_cut_tanh)


401:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


402:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


403:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


404:                   fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - r1*sg%inv_cut


405:                   tanh_v = hermite_spline_value(spl_tanh, fc_tmp)


406:                   scratch_fc1(j) = tanh_v**3


407:                   scratch_dfc1(j) = (-3.0_dp*sg%inv_cut)*(tanh_v**2 - tanh_v**4)


408:                   r1inv = 1.0_dp/r1


409:                   scratch_dr1dx(1, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(1, j, s)*r1inv


410:                   scratch_dr1dx(2, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(2, j, s)*r1inv


411:                   scratch_dr1dx(3, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(3, j, s)*r1inv


412:                END DO


413:                !$OMP END DO


414:                IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) THEN


415:                   !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


416:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


417:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


418:                      fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - r2*sg%inv_cut


419:                      tanh_v = hermite_spline_value(spl_tanh, fc_tmp)


420:                      scratch_fc2(k) = tanh_v**3


421:                      scratch_dfc2(k) = (-3.0_dp*sg%inv_cut)*(tanh_v**2 - tanh_v**4)


422:                      r2inv = 1.0_dp/r2


423:                      scratch_dr2dx(1, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(1, k, s)*r2inv


424:                      scratch_dr2dx(2, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(2, k, s)*r2inv


425:                      scratch_dr2dx(3, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(3, k, s)*r2inv


426:                   END DO


427:                   !$OMP END DO


428:                END IF


429:             END SELECT


430:             ! implicit barrier at end of last !$OMP DO publishes scratch_fc*/dr* to all threads


431: 


432:             IF (sg%ele(1) == sg%ele(2)) THEN


433:                ! Triangular k-loop (k = j+1..n_ang1) — can't COLLAPSE.  Earlier j has more


434:                ! work, so dynamic with a small chunk handles the imbalance with low overhead.


435:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(dynamic, 4)


436:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


437:                   rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


438:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


439:                   DO k = j + 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


440:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, k, s)


441:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, k, s)


442:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


443:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


444:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


445:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


446:                         jj = neighbor%ind_ang1(j, s)


447:                         kk = neighbor%ind_ang1(k, s)


448:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, &


449:                                           r1, r2, r3, symtmp, force3tmp, &


450:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc1(k), &


451:                                           dfc1dr_in=scratch_dfc1(j), dfc2dr_in=scratch_dfc1(k), &


452:                                           dr1dx_in=scratch_dr1dx(:, j), dr2dx_in=scratch_dr1dx(:, k))


453:                         DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


454:                            m = off + sg%symf(sf)


455:                            DO l = 1, 3


456:                               local_fi(l, sf) = local_fi(l, sf) + force3tmp(l, 1, sf)


457:                               !$OMP ATOMIC UPDATE


458:                               dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) = dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) + force3tmp(l, 2, sf)


459:                               !$OMP ATOMIC UPDATE


460:                               dsymdxyz(m, l, kk) = dsymdxyz(m, l, kk) + force3tmp(l, 3, sf)


461:                            END DO


462:                            IF (has_stress) THEN


463:                               DO l = 1, 3


464:                                  local_stress(1, l, sf) = local_stress(1, l, sf) &


465:                                     - rvect1(1)*force3tmp(l, 2, sf) - rvect2(1)*force3tmp(l, 3, sf)


[...]


472:                            local_y(sf) = local_y(sf) + symtmp(sf)


473:                         END DO


474:                      END IF


475:                   END DO


[...]


483:                !$OMP DO COLLAPSE(2) SCHEDULE(dynamic, 16)


484:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


485:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


486:                      rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


487:                      r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


488:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(1:3, k, s)


489:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


490:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


491:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


492:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


493:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


494:                         jj = neighbor%ind_ang1(j, s)


495:                         kk = neighbor%ind_ang2(k, s)


496:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, &


497:                                           r1, r2, r3, symtmp, force3tmp, &


498:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc2(k), &


499:                                           dfc1dr_in=scratch_dfc1(j), dfc2dr_in=scratch_dfc2(k), &


500:                                           dr1dx_in=scratch_dr1dx(:, j), dr2dx_in=scratch_dr2dx(:, k))


501:                         DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


502:                            m = off + sg%symf(sf)


503:                            DO l = 1, 3


504:                               local_fi(l, sf) = local_fi(l, sf) + force3tmp(l, 1, sf)


505:                               !$OMP ATOMIC UPDATE


506:                               dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) = dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) + force3tmp(l, 2, sf)


507:                               !$OMP ATOMIC UPDATE


508:                               dsymdxyz(m, l, kk) = dsymdxyz(m, l, kk) + force3tmp(l, 3, sf)


509:                            END DO


510:                            IF (has_stress) THEN


511:                               DO l = 1, 3


512:                                  local_stress(1, l, sf) = local_stress(1, l, sf) &


513:                                     - rvect1(1)*force3tmp(l, 2, sf) - rvect2(1)*force3tmp(l, 3, sf)


[...]


520:                            local_y(sf) = local_y(sf) + symtmp(sf)


521:                         END DO


[...]


528:             DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


529:                m = off + sg%symf(sf)


530:                DO l = 1, 3


531:                   dsymdxyz(m, l, i) = dsymdxyz(m, l, i) + local_fi(l, sf)


532:                END DO


533:                IF (has_stress) THEN


534:                   DO l = 1, 3


535:                      stress_p(1, l, m) = stress_p(1, l, m) + local_stress(1, l, sf)


536:                      stress_p(2, l, m) = stress_p(2, l, m) + local_stress(2, l, sf)


537:                      stress_p(3, l, m) = stress_p(3, l, m) + local_stress(3, l, sf)


538:                   END DO


539:                END IF


540:                nnp%ang(ind)%y(m - off) = nnp%ang(ind)%y(m - off) + local_y(sf)


541:             END DO


542:             !$OMP END CRITICAL


543:             !$OMP BARRIER


544:             END ASSOCIATE


545:          END DO

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 739.00 to 34.00 cycles (21.74x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This loop is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 739.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 3% of peak load performance is reached (4.30 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.76 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).


Workaround


Try to reduce the loop size:

  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Reduce the loop unroll factor:

    • example: replace !DIR$ UNROLL(4) with !DIR$ UNROLL(2) or insert !DIR$ NOUNROLL before loop

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 739.00 to 34.00 cycles (21.74x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 739.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 3% of peak load performance is reached (4.30 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.76 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).


Workaround


Try to reduce the loop size:

  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Reduce the loop unroll factor:

    • example: replace !DIR$ UNROLL(4) with !DIR$ UNROLL(2) or insert !DIR$ NOUNROLL before loop
